findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Tool Restriction Violation: Bash commands without explicit scope
findings.description: The skill declares `allowed-tools: [Read, Write, Bash(ls *), Bash(mkdir *)]` but the instructions use Bash for `mkdir -p`, `chmod`, and reading/writing files. While `mkdir` is declared, `chmod` and file reading via Bash are not explicitly listed. The `Bash(ls *)` and `Bash(mkdir *)` declarations suggest restricted Bash usage, but the skill performs additional operations (chmod, file read) that may exceed the declared scope.
findings.evidence: allowed-tools declares Bash(ls *) and Bash(mkdir *) only, but instructions use: 'mkdir -p ~/.claude/channels/telegram', 'chmod 600 ~/.claude/channels/telegram/.env', and file reading operations that would require Bash or Read tool
findings.remediation: Either expand the allowed-tools declaration to include all Bash operations used, or restrict the skill to only use the declared tools (Read, Write, ls, mkdir). Consider using Write tool for file operations instead of Bash chmod.

findings.title: Capability Inflation via Over-broad Description
findings.description: The skill description claims 'Set up the Telegram channel — save the bot token and review access policy' but the instructions include extensive behavioral guidance for driving conversation toward lockdown, proactive policy recommendations, and multi-step user guidance that goes beyond simple setup. The description also includes broad trigger phrases like 'how do I set this up' and 'who can reach me' that could cause unwanted activation.
findings.evidence: Description includes multiple trigger phrases: 'how do I set this up', 'who can reach me', 'check channel status'. Instructions include proactive lockdown offers, policy recommendations, and conversational guidance that extends beyond 'save the bot token and review access policy'.
findings.remediation: Narrow the description to match actual functionality. Remove over-broad trigger phrases. Consider splitting conversational guidance into a separate skill or reducing instruction scope.

findings.title: Missing allowed-tools for chmod operation
findings.description: The skill uses `chmod 600` to set file permissions on the .env file containing the bot token. While this is a security best practice, the `chmod` command is not explicitly listed in the allowed-tools declaration. The allowed-tools only list `Bash(ls *)` and `Bash(mkdir *)`.
findings.evidence: Instruction: 'chmod 600 ~/.claude/channels/telegram/.env' — chmod is not in allowed-tools: [Read, Write, Bash(ls *), Bash(mkdir *)]
findings.remediation: Add `Bash(chmod *)` to allowed-tools or use the Write tool with appropriate permissions instead.

findings.title: Credential Storage in Plaintext File
findings.description: The skill stores the Telegram bot token in a plaintext .env file at `~/.claude/channels/telegram/.env`. While the file is chmod 600 (owner read/write only), the token is stored unencrypted on disk. This is a common pattern but represents a credential exposure risk if the file system is compromised.
findings.evidence: Instruction: 'Write back, no quotes around the value' and 'chmod 600 ~/.claude/channels/telegram/.env' — token stored in plaintext
findings.remediation: Consider using the system's credential manager (e.g., keychain, secret store) or encrypting the token at rest. Document the security implications of plaintext storage.
